response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I am not someone who spends much time worrying about these modern academic concepts, but I understand what stereotype threat means from what my grandchildren have told me. It is this idea that when people are told they cannot do something because of who they are, whether woman or man or whatever background, they start believing it and perform worse. In science and mathematics fields especially, girls supposedly get nervous thinking everyone expects them to fail. Frankly, I think people make too much fuss about feelings these days. When I was young, nobody coddled us with concerns about our confidence. My parents had basic education, my father only finished primary school, and we simply got on with our work. I finished upper secondary school, raised five children after coming to Bari, and now at seventy-two I still work part-time delivering packages. You do what needs doing without complaining. That said, I am not completely dismissive. I have seen my own granddaughters struggle with mathematics teachers who clearly favored the boys in class, and that bothered me. If we are going to address this stereotype threat business, we should do it practically, not with endless talking and sensitivity sessions. Teachers need to treat all students the same, period. Give everyone the same challenges, the same expectations, the same consequences. Stop separating children into groups and making some feel special or victimized. When I play board games with my grandchildren, I do not let them win just to protect their feelings. They learn by trying hard and sometimes failing. The same principle applies to education. Set clear standards, enforce them consistently, and stop telling young people they are disadvantaged before they even start. If someone has genuine ability in science or mathematics, they will succeed if they work hard enough. The real problem is not stereotypes but lack of discipline and commitment. Address that first, and the rest takes care of itself.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 72-year-old woman with low openness and low agreeableness, suggesting resistance to new concepts and a direct, unsentimental approach. Her high conscientiousness indicates belief in hard work and discipline. High extraversion means she speaks confidently and directly. Her working-class background, upper secondary education, and practical occupation as a delivery driver suggest pragmatic, no-nonsense views. Her age and traditional values would make her somewhat skeptical of modern academic concepts while her experience raising five children and working provides practical perspective. The low agreeableness means she would be blunt and not particularly sympathetic to complaints about difficulties. She would acknowledge the issue exists based on personal observation but favor practical, traditional solutions over what she might see as coddling.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, pragmatic, somewhat dismissive of modern sensitivities, blunt, confident, grounded in personal experience and traditional values
